body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: On Stable Storage for Items in Concise Binary Object Representation (CBOR)
Concise Binary Object Representation (CBOR) Tags for IPv4 and IPv6 Addresses and Prefixes
Concise Binary Object Representation (CBOR) Tags for Time, Duration, and Period
A feature freezer for the Concise Data Definition Language (CDDL)
Concise Binary Object Representation (CBOR) Tags for Object Identifiers
Additional Control Operators for the Concise Data Definition Language (CDDL)

here is um like a seabor map um but in um in python uh there is um something called an ordered map which is a key to value mapping uh it behaves just like a hash table or an associative array um but the insertion order is important um the benefit of this is that uh it's gives you efficient iteration over all items um and uh in python at least it's fairly uh fairly compact uh next slide please um so yeah like i said in python it's the ordered dict class an implementation implementation detail of python's built-in dict is that it it shares the same underlying data structures originally that was purely coincidence but since i think python 3.8 that has been documented behavior in that the built-in dict preserves insertion order but the difference that order dict has is that it's got some extra methods that allow you to move things around um and also when you're comparing for equality the order is important whereas for the original dict uh it's not important uh next slide please uh similarly in in javascript uh there is a uh a map type [Music] which allows extended key types [Music] similar to how you can use like a python tuple as a as a key um you can do a similar thing with a javascript's map allows better iteration over the keys than the built-in object um i didn't know this um before i wrote this presentation but the it's possible to get key collisions with um object the object's prototype um next slide please um so in ruby um the hash is actually ordered by default since uh 1.9 um but it's not used for equality comparisons um so if you were using this in in ruby uh it would be up to the author whether you felt that the um the order of of your hash map was important um next slide please so this originally came to my attention when a user of uh our library um opened an issue saying hey when i serialize uh ordered um they just get serialized as as type 5 um which is um which is just a plain map um and if you're using a deterministic encoding then it reorders those keys so the original information is lost um it also means that that when decoding it becomes a plain dict uh when when instantiating the object um and so he requested you know a way to always decode maps as as order dicts but i suggested that we use a tag for it instead so that we could communicate to others that the order of this is important next slide please um so for uh for us and other maps are useful in a lot of environments where you actually want to have deterministic results but not necessarily in the way that the reordering uh done by by sibo's deterministic encoding
